summary: Gruber analyzes the Ninth Circuit's decision to stay the Epic v. Apple injunction that would have forced App Store changes. He argues the stay signals the injunction was on shaky legal footing from the start. Crucially, he corrects a widespread misconception: even if the injunction had taken effect, Apple made clear it intended to collect its 15-30% commission on all in-app digital purchases regardless of payment method. Apple's argument for the stay was that building infrastructure to track and collect commissions on non-IAP purchases would cost significant resources they couldn't recoup if they won on appeal. Gruber concludes that those hoping the injunction would eliminate Apple's commission entirely were always mistaken about what it actually required.
key_points: The Ninth Circuit stayed the injunction requiring Apple to allow external payment links, keeping the current IAP-only system in place pending appeal
Apple's legal team expressed strong confidence the injunction would not survive appellate review, noting similar anti-steering provisions are common across digital platforms
The injunction only required Apple to allow alternative payment processing — not to give up its commission, a distinction many commentators missed
Apple argued the stay was necessary because building commission-tracking infrastructure for non-IAP purchases would cause irreparable injury if they ultimately won on appeal
The widespread assumption that the injunction would let developers bypass Apple's commission entirely was never correct under the ruling's actual terms
money_quotes: This isn't quite 'game over', but I believe it's close.
Judge Yvonne Gonzalez Rogers's injunction mandating changes to the App Store seemed to be on shaky legal footing all along.

The injunction requires only that Apple allow other forms of payment processing, including links to the web — not that they aren't entitled to monetize the platform by charging a mandatory commission.
Apple has not previously charged a commission on purchases of digital content via buttons and links because such purchases have not been permitted.

There are a lot of people who really wanted this injunction to stick, under the premise that it would force Apple to open the App Store to third-party in-app purchasing for digital content without Apple taking any cut whatsoever.
That was never going to be the case, even if this injunction had gone into effect.
What was the point of the injunction then? you might ask. Good question.
key_insight: The Epic v. Apple injunction was widely misunderstood — it never would have eliminated Apple's commission, only changed the payment mechanism, making the stay largely academic for those hoping it would break Apple's revenue model.
